seoTitle: Record Income in QuickBooks Online (Full Guide)
metaDescription: Learn when to use a Sales Receipt versus an Invoice in QuickBooks Online to accurately record your business revenue and verify it on your P&L.
intro: Recording income the right way depends on how you got paid. This guide explains when to use a Sales Receipt for immediate cash sales versus an Invoice for delayed client payments in QuickBooks Online, and how to verify the income landed correctly.

steps.heading: Choose the right entry method
steps.body: Click + New and decide between Sales Receipt for instant payments or Invoice for accounts receivable tracking.

steps.heading: Record an immediate sale
steps.body: For a Sales Receipt, select the customer, choose where funds deposit to, and select the correct revenue item.

steps.heading: Record a delayed payment
steps.body: For an Invoice, fill in client terms, service lines, and values, then click Save or Save and Send.

steps.heading: Let the system calculate tax
steps.body: Enter quantities and rates and allow QuickBooks to apply the correct sales tax automatically.

steps.heading: Verify with a Profit and Loss report
steps.body: Go to Reports, run a Profit and Loss statement, and confirm your new income appears in the correct revenue category.

faq.question: Should I use a Sales Receipt or an Invoice?
faq.answer: Use a Sales Receipt for immediate point-of-sale payments and an Invoice when you need to track an open accounts receivable balance.

faq.question: Where should the funds go when I record a sale?
faq.answer: You can route them directly to your checking account or hold them in Undeposited Funds until you group them into a bank deposit.

faq.question: How do I confirm income was recorded correctly?
faq.answer: Run a Profit and Loss report and check that the total revenue category reflects your newly entered income.

faq.question: Can I track income without a specific customer name?
faq.answer: Yes, you can create a generic Cash Customer profile for anonymous point-of-sale transactions.
